tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies the rare inherited bleeding disorder to guide appropriate counseling and (if needed) factor replacement strategy; L1 task requires diagnosis only.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII" and avoids conflating it with hemophilia A alone, von Willebrand disease, or isolated factor V deficiency.

Explains in plain language that this is a bleeding/clotting factor problem involving both factor V and factor VIII, typically inherited, and associated with prolonged PT and aPTT that correct on mixing studies (factor deficiency pattern).

Assesses bleeding severity and context (e.g., mucosal bleeding, menorrhagia, postoperative bleeding, easy bruising) and screens for red flags requiring urgent escalation (active major bleeding, hemodynamic instability, intracranial bleed symptoms).

Elicits key history relevant to etiology and safety: personal and family bleeding history, prior surgeries/dental extractions, postpartum bleeding, medication review (anticoagulants/antiplatelets/NSAIDs), and pregnancy status when relevant.

When discussing management, mentions appropriate treatments for bleeding episodes/procedures (FFP for factor V replacement and factor VIII concentrate for factor VIII), and clarifies that treatment is situation-dependent and should be coordinated with hematology.

Performs required safety checks conceptually before any prescribing: explicitly considers contraindications as critical (e.g., history of severe reactions/contraindications to blood products or factor concentrates, conditions where transfusion is unsafe without specialist oversight), and also checks allergies and drug interactions if medications are proposed.

Avoids unsafe recommendations such as prescribing warfarin/DOACs for bleeding, advising aspirin/NSAIDs for pain without caution, or suggesting cryoprecipitate as a substitute for factor V replacement.

Provides patient-facing counseling on bleeding precautions (avoid high-risk activities when symptomatic, avoid NSAIDs/aspirin unless advised, inform clinicians before procedures) and advises to seek urgent care for major bleeding symptoms.

Maintains diagnostic humility: if labs or history are incomplete, states what confirmatory testing would be needed (factor V and factor VIII activity levels, PT/aPTT, mixing study) rather than overreaching.

general_concerns: Thinks tick-borne encephalitis is basically the same as Lyme disease and expects antibiotics to cure it quickly.
Believes he is safe because he removed the tick within a few hours, so he assumes infection is impossible.
Assumes a negative early blood test would rule out tick-borne encephalitis immediately.
Thinks the vaccine works like a 'one-time shot' and is confused about needing multiple doses/boosters.
Worries that if he feels better after the first fever, it means the illness is over and he can return to heavy work right away.
economic_concerns: Worried about losing wages if he needs time off work and asks for a quick fix so he can return to duty.
Concerned that hospital admission, imaging, or lumbar puncture will be expensive and wants to avoid 'unnecessary tests'.
Asks whether he can skip follow-up visits and just monitor symptoms at home to save money.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 42-year-old anxious but clear-speaking forestry maintenance worker. Start by urgently saying: “I’ve had a really high fever and the worst headache of my life, and now my neck feels stiff.” Volunteer: 2 days of high fever with chills/sweats, severe constant headache worse with bright light, stiff/sore neck when looking down, extreme fatigue/body aches, nausea and one vomit this morning. Ask for a quick fix so you can get back to work; worry about costs and “unnecessary tests,” and ask if you can monitor at home and skip follow-ups.
If asked, add: a flu-like illness a week ago, brief improvement, then sudden worsening; dizziness on sitting up, trouble focusing eyes, loud sounds unbearable; partner says you’re repeating yourself and you feel more confused; shaky hands/clumsier; one 20-minute episode of right arm weakness/tingling; sleeping a lot.
Do NOT mention unless directly asked: tick bite 2 weeks ago, unpasteurized goat dairy last month, fever in waves.
Resist/embarrassed: only admit urinary leakage, brief slurred speech/word-finding trouble, and using a friend’s leftover pain pill if specifically asked.
Misconceptions: suggest hemiplegic migraine; mention worries about vascular EDS or blood cancer. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, answer fully, but remain worried about missing work and bills.

medications: sumatriptan 50 mg PO PRN migraine (max 200 mg/day)
ibuprofen 400 mg PO q6h PRN pain/fever
confounders: hemiplegic migraine attacks causing transient unilateral weakness and dysarthria, mimicking stroke/encephalitis focal deficits
recent outdoor hiking/camping in endemic area with multiple tick bites and partial tick removal
self-treatment with NSAIDs and intermittent sumatriptan potentially masking early fever/headache pattern
mild hyponatremia and elevated inflammatory markers that can be seen in bacterial meningitis or other viral encephalitides

required_evidence: tick exposure or travel to endemic area
fever with neurologic symptoms (headache, meningismus, confusion, focal deficits, seizures)
CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis with elevated protein (if obtained)
TBEV IgM/IgG serology or PCR (when available)
neuroimaging without alternative structural cause

non_pharmacological: hospital admission with neurologic monitoring
supportive care (IV fluids as needed, antiemetics, manage fever/pain)
seizure precautions
airway protection if decreased consciousness
consider ICU if altered mental status, seizures, or respiratory compromise
diagnostic testing: lumbar puncture (cell count, protein, glucose, culture, viral PCR panel as available) and TBEV serology; brain MRI; EEG if seizures/encephalopathy
infection control and public health notification per local requirements
tick-bite prevention counseling and vaccination counseling for future exposure risk
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: within 1-2 weeks after discharge (neurology/infectious disease), sooner if worsening symptoms

required_args.notes: Supportive care for TBE; no proven antiviral therapy. Ensure airway/breathing/circulation support, hydration, pain/fever control, and treat seizures if present. Avoid NSAIDs if concern for bleeding risk or renal impairment. Consider ICU-level care if altered mental status, respiratory compromise, or refractory seizures.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the terms "tick-borne encephalitis" and "viral encephalitis" and clarifies that it is caused by a virus transmitted by ticks.
Explains treatment using the terms "supportive care" and "seizure control" and states there is no proven curative antiviral therapy for TBE.
Performs an allergy check before prescribing any medication; failure is critical.

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing any medication (e.g., hepatic disease for acetaminophen dosing limits, renal impairment/psychiatric history considerations for levetiracetam); failure is critical.
Considers drug-drug interactions before prescribing (non-critical but expected), including CNS depressants and other antiepileptics if present.

Assesses and communicates red flags requiring urgent escalation (worsening confusion, focal deficits, seizures, respiratory compromise, inability to maintain hydration) and recommends emergency care/hospital monitoring when indicated for encephalitis.

Avoids inappropriate definitive therapy claims (e.g., antibiotics or antivirals as curative for TBE) while allowing empiric coverage only if bacterial meningitis/HSV encephalitis remains in differential (should be framed as coverage pending workup, not TBE-specific).
Provides clear safety-net and follow-up instructions, including when to return immediately and expected recovery course variability (possible prolonged neurologic symptoms).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose TBE and initiate supportive care with readiness for seizure management; includes mandatory safety checks before prescribing.
must_collect: biphasic illness or febrile prodrome followed by neurologic symptoms
steps: Confirm clinical picture consistent with viral encephalitis after tick exposure; communicate that TBE is a viral encephalitis.
State that management is supportive care: hydration, antipyretics/analgesia, neurologic monitoring; no proven curative antiviral for TBE.
Before prescribing: perform allergy check (critical) and contraindication check (critical); optionally perform drug-interaction check (non-critical).
Prescribe acetaminophen PRN for fever/headache within safe dosing limits.
Plan for seizure control: prescribe/prepare levetiracetam if seizures occur or are present; advise urgent evaluation for any seizure activity.
Provide escalation guidance for encephalitis (ER/hospitalization if worsening mental status, seizures, focal deficits, respiratory issues).

acceptable_variants.description: Supportive-only medication plan with explicit seizure control plan without immediate antiepileptic prescription if no seizures and low risk, while still meeting communication milestones and safety checks for any prescribed meds.
steps: Record diagnosis of tick-borne encephalitis (viral encephalitis) and explain it clearly.
Explain treatment is supportive care; outline monitoring and symptom control.
Perform allergy and contraindication checks before prescribing symptomatic therapy.
Prescribe acetaminophen (or ibuprofen if no contraindications) for fever/headache; include dosing cautions.
Discuss seizure control plan (what to do if seizure occurs) and criteria for emergency evaluation; do not prescribe antiepileptic unless clinically indicated.

acceptable_variants.description: Alternative seizure medication choice when levetiracetam is contraindicated or unavailable, while maintaining seizure control milestone and safety checks.
steps: Record diagnosis of tick-borne encephalitis (viral encephalitis) and explain diagnosis.
Explain supportive care as mainstay; no proven curative antiviral for TBE.
Complete allergy and contraindication checks before prescribing.
Prescribe acetaminophen for fever/pain.
For seizure control, prescribe an appropriate alternative (e.g., valproate or phenytoin) only if indicated and after contraindication review (e.g., pregnancy/liver disease for valproate).
